stem: Every ideal of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) has the form \( (d) \) for a divisor \( d \) of \( n \), and distinct divisors give distinct ideals. Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). How many ideals does \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) have?
solution: Ideals of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) correspond bijectively to positive divisors of \( n \). Since \( n = [[p]]^{[[a]]}[[q]]^{[[b]]} \), the number of divisors (hence ideals) is \( ([[a]]+1)([[b]]+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the raw exponents instead of increasing each by one first, as the divisor-counting formula requires.
This forgets to count the ideal \( (1) = \mathbb{Z}_n \) itself (or equivalently \( (n) = \{0\} \)) among the divisors.
This adds the shifted exponent counts instead of multiplying them.
This is \( n \) itself, not the number of its divisors.
This is one more than the correct count of ideals.
This is one fewer than the correct count of ideals.
This is two more than the correct count of ideals.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[d]] \cdot [[m]] \), and let \( I = ([[d]]) \) be the principal ideal of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) generated by \( [[d]] \) (all multiples of \( [[d]] \) inside \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \)). How many elements does the ideal \( I \) have?
solution: The multiples of \( [[d]] \) inside \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) are \( 0, [[d]], 2\cdot[[d]], \dots, (n/[[d]] - 1)\cdot[[d]] \), giving \( n/[[d]] = [[m]] = [[answer]] \) elements in \( I \).
distractor_rationales: This is the GENERATOR \( d \) itself, not the number of elements the ideal it generates actually contains.
This is the size of the whole ring \( \mathbb{Z}_n \), not just the ideal \( I \).
This subtracts \( d \) from \( n \) for no reason connected to counting multiples of \( d \).
This guesses the ideal is always exactly half the ring, which is only a coincidence for some moduli.
This is one more than the correct ideal size.
This is one fewer than the correct ideal size.
This adds an unrelated offset \( d \) to the correct ideal size.

stem: In the ring of integers \( \mathbb{Z} \), every ideal is principal. The sum of two ideals \( (a) + (b) = \{ax + by : x,y \in \mathbb{Z}\} \) is itself principal, generated by \( \gcd(a,b) \). What is the positive generator of \( ([[a]]) + ([[b]]) \)?
solution: The sum \( (a)+(b) \) is the smallest ideal containing both \( a \) and \( b \), which (in \( \mathbb{Z} \)) is \( (\gcd(a,b)) \). Here \( \gcd([[a]], [[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the generator of the INTERSECTION \( (a) \cap (b) \), not the sum \( (a)+(b) \); sums use the gcd, intersections use the lcm.
This is the product \( ab \), unrelated to either the sum or intersection ideal.
This adds the two generators directly, rather than computing their gcd.
This takes the absolute difference of the two generators, unrelated to the gcd.
This is one more than the correct generator.
This is one fewer than the correct generator.
This is two more than the correct generator.

stem: In the ring of integers \( \mathbb{Z} \), the intersection of two principal ideals \( (a) \cap (b) \) (the common multiples of both \( a \) and \( b \)) is itself principal, generated by \( \operatorname{lcm}(a,b) \). What is the positive generator of \( ([[a]]) \cap ([[b]]) \)?
solution: The intersection \( (a)\cap(b) \) consists of the common multiples of \( a \) and \( b \), so (in \( \mathbb{Z} \)) it equals \( (\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)) \). Here \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]], [[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the generator of the SUM \( (a)+(b) \), not the intersection; intersections use the lcm, sums use the gcd.
This is the raw product \( ab \), which only equals the lcm when \( a,b \) are coprime.
This adds the two generators directly, rather than computing their lcm.
This halves the raw product \( ab \) for no valid reason connected to the lcm.
This is one more than the correct generator.
This is one fewer than the correct generator.
This is two more than the correct generator.
